title: Gmina Wilanów

infobox.value: [Wilanów Palace](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace "Wilanów Palace"), Poster Museum, palace park

(Polish pronunciation: [[vʲiˈlanuf]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q809_(pol)-Olaf-Wilan%C3%B3w.wav "File:LL-Q809 (pol)-Olaf-Wilanów.wav")) is a [district](/wiki/Dzielnica "Dzielnica") of the city of [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"). It is home to historic [Wilanów Palace](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace "Wilanów Palace"), the "Polish [Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles")," and second home to various [Polish kings](/wiki/List_of_Polish_rulers "List of Polish rulers"). Wilanów is home to many villas and, despite being relatively far away from the city center, the district ranks among the most expensive in Warsaw.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** ["Powierzchnia i ludność w przekroju terytorialnym w 2019 roku. Tabl. 21 Powierzchnia, ludność oraz lokaty według gmi"](https://stat.gov.pl/obszary-tematyczne/ludnosc/ludnosc/powierzchnia-i-ludnosc-w-przekroju-terytorialnym-w-2019-roku,7,16.html). *stat.gov.pl*. Główny Urząd Statystyczny. Retrieved 2 September 2020.

[Augustyn Wincenty Locci, the Author of Wilanów Village](http://www.wilanow-palac.pl/augustyn_wincenty_locci_the_author_of_wilanow_palace.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20211110113420/https://www.wilanow-palac.pl/augustyn_wincenty_locci_the_author_of_wilanow_palace.html) 2021-11-10 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") at the Wilanów Palace Museum

**Wilanów** (Polish pronunciation: [[vʲiˈlanuf]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q809_(pol)-Olaf-Wilan%C3%B3w.wav "File:LL-Q809 (pol)-Olaf-Wilanów.wav")) is a [district](/wiki/Dzielnica "Dzielnica") of the city of [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"). It is home to historic [Wilanów Palace](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace "Wilanów Palace"), the "Polish [Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles")," and second home to various [Polish kings](/wiki/List_of_Polish_rulers "List of Polish rulers"). Wilanów is home to many villas and, despite being relatively far away from the city center, the district ranks among the most expensive in Warsaw.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wilan%C3%B3w&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The first mentions of a settlement in the area can be traced to the 13th century, when a village named *Milanów* was founded by the [Benedictine](/wiki/Benedictine "Benedictine") monastery of [Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock "Płock"). In 1338 it became a private property of the [Dukes of Mazovia](/wiki/Dukes_of_Mazovia "Dukes of Mazovia") and in 1378 Prince [Janusz I of Warsaw](/wiki/Janusz_I_of_Warsaw "Janusz I of Warsaw") gave it to one of his servants. It was he who established the first mansion and a chapel in the village. His descendants adopted the name *Milanowski*, after the name of the village.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Zygmunt_Vogel_-_Pa%C5%82ac_w_Wilanowie_od_strony_parku_%28akw_1791%29.jpg/250px-Zygmunt_Vogel_-_Pa%C5%82ac_w_Wilanowie_od_strony_parku_%28akw_1791%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zygmunt_Vogel_-_Pa%C5%82ac_w_Wilanowie_od_strony_parku_(akw_1791).jpg)

*[Wilanów Palace](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace "Wilanów Palace") as seen from the park* by [Zygmunt Vogel](/wiki/Zygmunt_Vogel "Zygmunt Vogel"), 1791-92.

In the 17th century the village was bought by the family of [Stanisław Leszczyński](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Leszczy%C5%84ski "Stanisław Leszczyński"), who started the construction of a new palace; however, the works were stopped by [The Deluge](/wiki/The_Deluge_(Polish_history) "The Deluge (Polish history)") when the forces of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") captured the area and plundered it completely. In 1676 the depopulated village was bought by King [Jan III Sobieski](/wiki/Jan_III_Sobieski "Jan III Sobieski"). By his order, [Tylman van Gameren](/wiki/Tylman_van_Gameren "Tylman van Gameren") and Augustyn Wincenty Locci erected the new [baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque")-style palace[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) and St. Anne's Church. Initially the palace was named *Villa Nova* (*New Village*), to distinguish it from the nearby village of *Stara Wieś* (*Old Village*). However, soon the name was polonised to Wilanów, similar to the former name Milanów.

Thanks to the proximity of both the kings' summer residence and the city of [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), Wilanów has for ages been a suburb of Warsaw and a popular holiday spot for Polish magnates. It was also the final point on the historical [Royal Road](/wiki/Royal_Road "Royal Road"). In the 18th century, the palace became the property of [Hetman](/wiki/Hetman "Hetman") [Adam Mikołaj Sieniawski](/wiki/Adam_Miko%C5%82aj_Sieniawski "Adam Mikołaj Sieniawski"). His widow, [Elżbieta Sieniawska](/wiki/El%C5%BCbieta_Lubomirska_(1669%E2%80%931729) "Elżbieta Lubomirska (1669–1729)"), joined the village with the nearby villages of [Kabaty](/wiki/Kabaty "Kabaty"), [Powsin](/wiki/Powsin "Powsin") and [Wolica](/wiki/Wolica,_Warsaw "Wolica, Warsaw"). After that both the so-called *key* of villages (group of villages run together by a common owner) and the palace changed hands several times, with each new owner changing something in the look of the palace.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Tortoiseshell_cabinet_Sobieski.JPG/250px-Tortoiseshell_cabinet_Sobieski.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tortoiseshell_cabinet_Sobieski.JPG)

[Tortoise-shell](/wiki/Tortoiseshell_material "Tortoiseshell material") [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(furniture) "Cabinet (furniture)") of [John III Sobieski](/wiki/John_III_Sobieski "John III Sobieski"), [looted](/wiki/Looting "Looting") by the Germans from the [Wilanów Palace](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace "Wilanów Palace") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In 1863, after the fall of the [January Uprising](/wiki/January_Uprising "January Uprising"), the [Russian](/wiki/Russia "Russia") authorities introduced a new administrative division, stripping the *key* of the rest of villages and making Wilanów a capital of all the communes located south of it. The area became one of the most important providers of food for ever-growing [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") and in 1890 a horse-drawn railway was opened for transport of grain and passengers. The line linking Wilanów with [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw")'s Lublin Union Square proved to be a major success and in 1892 the line was extended and started to run steam-drawn trains. Also, new railway stations were built on both ends of the line.

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the palaces in [Wilanów](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace "Wilanów Palace"), [Natolin](/wiki/Natolin "Natolin") and Morysin were [nationalised](/wiki/Nationalisation "Nationalisation") by the new communist authorities of Poland. The former was converted into a museum and in one of the palace's barracks a Museum of [Posters](/wiki/Poster "Poster") was opened on 4 June 1968. It is probably the only such museum in Europe. In 1951 Wilanów was incorporated into [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"). Initially a separate unit of administrative division, in 1976 it was joined with the borough of [Mokotów](/wiki/Mokot%C3%B3w "Mokotów"). In 1994 it became a separate unit of administrative division and the following year it became one of the boroughs of Warsaw.

Currently, the surrounding area is home to many expatriates living in Warsaw, as this area is home to many international schools. However, in 2000, the American School of Warsaw moved about 6 km out, to the suburb of [Konstancin-Jeziorna](/wiki/Konstancin-Jeziorna "Konstancin-Jeziorna").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace.jpg/960px-Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace.jpg)

[Wilanów Palace](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace "Wilanów Palace"), view from the Royal Gardens
